title: Lecture2 Part 1 Hedging 2024T1
body:
Lecture 2 – Part 1: Hedging with Forward and Futures

Hull Chapter 3  What is hedging?

•The objective is to reduce risk exposure of a portfolio or strategy to certain underlying risks.

•It is similar to buying an insurance policy, except that: • We need to construct the hedge from tradeable instruments. • The portfolio of financial assets is the insurance equivalent of an insurance contract

•Such an “insurance portfolio” is known as a hedge

How does hedging relates to pricing?

•Hedging is similar to replicating. If you know how to replicate, then you know how to hedge.

Replication: • Find two portfolios that perfectly mimic each other (cash flows).

Hedging: •You have an existing position and you remove some risk by taking an offsetting position of the replicating portfolio or something close to it (in practice just a futures contract) •The goal of hedging is to reduce risk, not to make profit or find arbitrage-free prices

Why is hedging important?

• Often investors want to get exposure to certain risks but not others. • If we are stock analysts, we may have superior information about a certain stock. • We are less likely to have superior information about the market as a whole. • We may want to place a bet on the stock without exposing ourselves to market risk.

• Companies may want to hedge certain financial risks that have little to do with they core business • A Sydney cab company may know about smart shortcuts and good places to pick up customers. • It is unlikely to know how petrol prices will evolve over time. • The company may want to hedge some costs incurred due fluctuations in oil prices. • Generally, the higher the fluctuations in net profits the lower a company valuation is…  Long and short hedges

•A long hedge if you need to buy the asset (input to our business). It involves a long position in the futures market • “Lock in” a buy price (futures price) through a futures contract.

•A short hedge if you sell the asset (producer). It involves a short position in the futures market. • “Lock in” a sell price (futures price) through a futures contract

Perfect hedge

• The problem is relatively trivial.

• Suppose we know that we will have to sell 1 ton of wheat in one year time. • Take a short position in a futures contract written on 1 ton of wheat and with one year maturity. • Cash flows:

• 𝑆𝑇 + 𝐹0 – 𝑆𝑇 = 𝐹0 which is known today!!! • Since all price risk is gone, this is a perfect hedge.

Perfect hedges are only found in dreams …

In practice, perfect hedging is rarely possible.

•Our exposure (quantity produced or revenues received from sales in one currency) itself is often unknown • The farmer does not know how large wheat harvest will be… • L'Oréal does not know its USD revenues next year…

•Sometimes, there is often no futures contracts written on the underlying we want to hedge • Crude oil contracts v.s. petrol at the station • Gold, and other commodity grade that may differ from the one produced.

•Futures contracts mature on specific dates each month • It is unlikely to find a futures contract that matures on the same day that the wheat being harvest and sold… the revenue received in USD  Mismatched maturities

•The futures price and the spot price generally differ prior to the delivery date

•Since the two prices converge at T, a hedger does not need to worry about the difference when her exposure matched exactly the maturity of the futures contract

•If the two maturities do not match, our final cash flows depends on the difference when we unwind the hedge (close the position).

Basis

•The basis at time t, 𝑏𝑡 , is defined as the difference between the spot price, 𝑆𝑡 , and the futures price, 𝐹𝑡 , at that time:

𝑏𝑡 = 𝑆𝑡 −𝐹𝑡

•Sometimes (but not in this course) you see the basis defined as 𝐹𝑡 − 𝑆𝑡

•Because we don’t know what the basis will be when we close our hedge, we do not know our total cash flows.

•This risk is known as basis risk

Example: Basis risk

• Suppose a futures contract on oranges runs from times 0 and T

• We are standing at time 𝑡1 and will harvest one ton of oranges at 𝑡2

• At 𝑡1 we set up a short hedge, i.e., take a short position in the futures contract, in order to lock in the sell price at 𝑡2 • The futures price is 𝐹1 at 𝑡1

• At 𝑡2 we close the position by taking an offsetting long position in the same futures contract • The futures price is 𝐹2 at 𝑡2 • At 𝑡2 we sell the oranges produced at the prevailing spot price 𝑆2

• Our cash flow at 𝑡2 is 𝐶𝐹2 = +𝑆2 + 𝐹1 – 𝐹2 = 𝐹1 + 𝑏2

Example: Basis risk

• Since 𝑏2 is unknown at 𝑡1 , 𝐶𝐹2 is uncertain (risky)

• This works in both directions: long and short hedge

• Suppose instead that we want to buy oranges at 𝑡2 • We set up a long hedge • Take a long position in the futures contract at 𝑡1 , at futures price 𝐹1 • At 𝑡2 , close the position at futures price 𝐹2 and buy the asset for the spot price 𝑆2 • 𝐶𝐹2 = −𝑆2 − 𝐹1 + 𝐹2 = −𝐹1 − 𝑏2

Is the basis risky?

•Typically, yes •We will show (in Part 2) that the futures price at 𝑡2 is

•This means that the basis will be

•The basis is proportional to the spot price •In practice the cost-of-carry and convenience yield are uncertain • Storage costs may change • Interest rates may change • Expected dividends may change • Convenience yields may vary  Mismatched assets

•When there is no futures contract written on the asset we want to hedge, we need to use something similar • hedge a petrol exposure in the crude oil market • hedge a zinc exposure in the copper market

•Recall that the basis is the difference between the futures price of the contract we use and the spot price of the asset we want to hedge • If the futures contract is written on some other assets that the one hedged it introduces an additional basis risk • Let’s denote the spot price of the asset on which the futures contract is written by S* and the spot price of the asset we want to hedge by S

Mismatched assets

•Rewrite the 𝑡2 cash flow from the short hedge as

𝐶𝐹2 = 𝑆2 + 𝐹1 – 𝐹2 ∗ ∗ = 𝑆2 + 𝐹1 – 𝐹2 +𝑆2 - 𝑆2 ∗ ∗ = 𝐹1 +(𝑆2 – 𝐹2 ) + (𝑆2 – 𝑆2 )

•We can attribute the cash flows at time 𝑡2 to three sources • 𝐹1 is the futures price we locked in at 𝑡1 • In a perfect hedge this would be the only part of the cash flow ∗ • (𝑆2 − 𝐹2 ) is basis risk coming from mismatched maturities ∗ • (𝑆2 − 𝑆2 ) is basis risk coming from mismatched assets

How do we deal with basis risk?

•In practice we cannot hedge all risks

•When hedging, we are substituting basis risk for price risk • We get exposed to relative rather than absolute price movements

•We can typically hedge a large part of the risk by setting up a hedging strategy in some smart way • What is the optimal way to do that? • How much risk are we left with?

How many futures contracts should we buy to hedge a given position?

•Suppose we want to hedge 𝑄𝐴 units of the asset and that one futures contract is a claim for 𝑄𝐹 units of the (same) asset • A reasonable guess is that we should sell 𝑄𝐴 /𝑄𝐹 contracts • For now, let’s assume that the contract size is one and that we want to hedge one unit of the asset • 𝑄𝐴 = 𝑄𝐹 = 1 • We will relax this assumption later

•How many futures contracts (if each is assumed to be a claim on one unit of the asset) should we sell to hedge one unit of the asset?

The hedge ratio

•Suppose we know that we will sell 1 unit of petrol in 3 months time • We are worried about oil price decrease.

•Given 𝑄𝐹 = 1 , should we buy one contract? • If all shocks affect the spot price and the futures price in the same way, it might be a good idea • If the shocks tend to have half the effect on the futures price as on the spot price, we need to “scale up” our futures position by buying 2 contracts

•We call this “scaling factor” hedge ratio and denote it h

∗ •We are interested in the optimal hedge ratio, ℎ • This is the choice of h that minimizes basis risk  Optimal hedge ratio

•Suppose we know we will sell one unit of the asset at time 𝑡2

•We set up a short hedge by taking a short position in h futures contracts at time 𝑡1 for delivery at time T

•At time 𝑡2 we close the position by taking an offsetting long position in h futures contracts

•At time 𝑡2 we also sell the asset for the prevailing spot price 𝑆2

Optimal hedge ratio

• What are our 𝑡2 cash flows, 𝐶𝐹2?

• If we had a perfect hedge, which is always what we are aiming for, 𝐶𝐹2 would be constant.

• As it is, 𝐶𝐹2 is stochastic because 𝑆2 and 𝐹2 are stochastic. Hence, we are unable to achieve a perfect hedge. • Our hedging objective is to minimize the variation (variance) of 𝐶𝐹2 . • This is equivalent to minimize risk of 𝐶𝐹2 • Var(𝐶𝐹2) can be written as

𝑽𝒂𝒓 𝑪𝑭𝟐 = 𝑽𝒂𝒓 𝑺𝟐 + 𝒉𝑭𝟏 − 𝒉𝑭𝟐 𝑽𝒂𝒓 𝑪𝑭𝟐 = 𝑽𝒂𝒓 𝑺𝟐 − 𝑺𝟏 + 𝑺𝟏 + 𝒉𝑭𝟏 − 𝒉𝑭𝟐 𝑽𝒂𝒓 𝑪𝑭𝟐 = 𝑽𝒂𝒓 ∆𝑺 + 𝑺𝟏 − 𝒉∆𝑭 𝑽𝒂𝒓 𝑪𝑭𝟐 = 𝑽𝒂𝒓 ∆𝑺 − 𝒉∆𝑭

• where 𝑺𝟏 is known when entering a futures position.  Optimal hedge ratio

• We want to choose the hedge ratio, h, so as to minimize this risk • The optimal hedge ratio, h*, sets the first derivative to zero

• For every asset we want to hedge, we should sell ℎ∗ claims in the futures market

• If we want to hedge 𝑄𝐴 units of assets and each futures contract gives a claim on 𝑄𝐹 units we multiply the “per claim” ratio, ℎ∗ , by 𝑄𝐴 /𝑄𝐹 to get the optimal ∗ number of contracts to sell, 𝑁

Interpreting the optimal hedge ratio

∗ • The expression for ℎ that we derived can be interpreted as a beta

• This is the value of β in an ordinary least-squares (OLS) regression of ΔS on ΔF

• Recall that this is the best fit of a line of a scatter plot of changes in the spot price against changes in the futures price

Interpreting the hedge ratio

•This is intuitive • β measures the average change in ΔS when ΔF changes by 1 • If β = 2, a change of 1 in ΔF tends to lead to a change of 2 in ΔS • We need to own 2 futures contracts to track changes in spot price.

•Suppose we had to construct a very poor hedge • We hedged a potato exposure with blueberry futures • β still gives us the average effect • Clearly the hedge will be poor…but perhaps better than not hedging at all.

•Recall that 𝑅 in a linear regression measures the fit

Interpreting the hedge ratio

• 𝑅 2 is the part of the variation in ΔS that can be “explained” by variations in ΔF

• 𝑅 in our regression can be interpreted as the fraction of risk that is hedged away

• We refer to this as the hedge effectiveness

• Recall that 𝑅 2 = 𝜌2 in a univariate linear regression

• The OLS interpretation of the hedge ratio is empirically important

• When hedging in practice we want to find ℎ∗

• We can do it by regressing past changes in the spot price on past changes in the futures price

• Since the Δ we are concerned with is from time 𝑡1 to time 𝑡2 , our data should have a similar time discretization. 23  Tailing the hedge for futures contracts

• Suppose the following: • There is no mismatch in maturities and assets, i.e., there is no basis risk • We want to hedge an exposure of one unit of the asset using futures contract • The futures are marked to market once, at time 0 < 𝑡 < 𝑇

• We want to explicitly take into account that the interest from gains and losses accrues in the margin account after marking the contract to market • Specifically, any gain (loss) in the futures position at time t is not offset by a corresponding loss (gain) in the spot market until time T

Tailing the hedge for future contracts

• When calculating the optimal number of contracts to buy, 𝑁 ∗ , we adjust for contract size by multiplying the scaling factor with 𝑄𝐴 /𝑄𝐹 as before

• The optimal number of contracts becomes the dollar value to be hedged divided by the dollar value of one futures contract.

What if there is basis risk?

Tailing the hedge

• Now let’s reintroduce basis risk • As before, we enter the contract at 𝑡1 > 0 and our exposure is at 𝑡2 < 𝑇 • The futures contracts are marked to market one day after 𝑡1 (and every day after that) • We need to adjust our position both for the basis risk (using ℎ∗ ) and for the daily settlement of the futures (using x) • The optimal number of contracts to enter into becomes

• In practice: • Always scale the hedge by the optimal hedge ratio, ℎ∗ • When using forward to set up the hedge, we don’t worry about marking-to-market and simply scale the hedge by the relative size of the contracts, 𝑄𝐴 /𝑄𝐹 • When using futures to set up the hedge, we scale the hedge by the relative value of the contracts, 𝑉𝐴 /𝑉𝐹

Group assignment 1

• Excel questions: • Margin Calls • Minimum Variance Hedge Ratio

• 1-4 students • Release in week 3 • Due in week 5

Group assignment 1

• Submission • Fill in your answers on the GA1 Answers in Moodle, and upload your excel work in the excel turn in. • Grading: • Grading approach: Full credit within 1 standard deviation of answer; 75% credit within 2 standard deviations of answer; 50% credit with 3 standard deviation. • I will also check your excel work for uniqueness. • If you are not able to submit your work on time, you need to discuss this prior to the deadline. • If you miss the deadline, your work will not be graded.

• Tips: • Remember to hit SUBMIT and not just save. • All work must be your own (or your group). • Choose one group member to submit all answers and excel • If you work in a group, fill in their names on the GA1 Answers.  Exercise questions
